items.q: How do you add the column vectors (3,4) and (1,-2)?
items.correct: Add the components separately to get (4, 2).
items.random_wrong: Add all four numbers to get a single value, 6.
items.why: Students try to multiply or combine into one number instead of adding components separately.

items.q: How is the magnitude of vector (3,4) found?
items.correct: Using Pythagoras: the square root of (3 squared + 4 squared) = 5.
items.why: Learners add or multiply components instead of using the Pythagorean formula for length.

items.q: What does multiplying a vector by a scalar do?
items.correct: It multiplies each component by the scalar, changing the vector's length while keeping its direction (reversed if negative).
items.misconception: It only changes the first component.
items.random_wrong: It rotates the vector by 90 degrees.
items.why: Students apply the scalar to one component only, instead of scaling every component.
The answer examiners want is: (4, 2). “(2, 2)” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: 5. “12” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Multiply vector (2, -3) by 4. What is the new x-component?
items.why: Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
